learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of contemporary practices of foreign policy formation and implementation
Evaluate the origins, role, and efficacy of tools and practices of diplomacy
Apply theories of international relations and foreign policy to particular case studies

assessments.task_description:
Policy Recommendation: 2 x A4 page Ministerial Policy Recommendation (up to 1000 words).

Students will be asked to choose a country and a key foreign policy issue they are interested in (concerning that country) and recommend a policy change to the Foreign Minister in an area that falls under the Foreign Minister’s jurisdiction. The policy recommendation will need to consider the following in both the briefing itself and recommendations:

- Briefly outline current policy and rationale behind it;

- Clearly outline the change and the rationale for it;

- Note other key players and their likely reactions;

- Note like-minded states in the international system with whom the country concerned could potentially build a coalition;

- Outline domestic implications (eg costs);

- Note key domestic state-holders and their concerns.

In-text references are not appropriate for this piece of assessment, but you should ensure that you include a bibliography of sources that you consulted in preparing the assessment. Submission via Turnitin (via Blackboard) by 2pm Friday 28 August. Example policy recommendations, and a document of tips / frequently asked questions, will be made available on Blackboard.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will submit a 3000 word research essay on a foreign policy case study, with questions to be posted on Blackboard in the first weeks of semester. This will be due on Friday 25th September, 2pm. Excess word length (over 10%) will be penalised. 

Questions will be made available on Blackboard or students can choose a question of their own in consultation with Matt. In the case of negotiating an alternative essay question, the topic and wording of the question must be agreed with Matt by 26th August. Students will be required to cite at least two (2) sources from the course reading list — failure to do so will incur a penalty of 10% = 4.5 marks.

Essays must be submitted through Turnitin (through Blackboard), an essay criteria sheet will be made available on Blackboard.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will sit an in-class, 90 minute examination on Wednesday 14th October at 8am. This examination will be composed of 30 short answer questions (worth 1 mark each). These questions will be correct / incorrect answers, hence criteria will not be available on Blackboard.

18 questions will be from the first five (5) foundational seminars of the semester on the topics: introduction to foreign policy (2 questions) traditional theories of foreign policy (4 questions); critical theories of foreign policy (4 questions); the processes of making foreign policy (4 questions); and practices of diplomacy (4 questions). The remaining 12 questions will come from the case study seminar weeks on the topics: China and the South China Sea (3 questions); US and Cuban Missile Crisis (3 questions); Australia and ODA (3 questions); Turkiye and the West (3 questions).

No material or devices (laptops, smart watches or phones) will be allowed for this exam, students are to bring writing material (i.e. pens) to complete the exam on the written exam script provided.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: NA - exam scripts will be collected from the exam venue
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 1 - Introduction

  • Introduction to the course and to each other
  • What is Foreign Policy?
  • The Development of Foreign Policy Analysis
  • Structure, Agency and States
  • The structure of the course, assessment, activities and resources

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 1 - Introduction Introduction to the course and to each other What is Foreign Policy? The Development of Foreign Policy Analysis Structure, Agency and States The structure of the course, assessment, activities and resources

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 2: Traditional Theories of Foreign Policy

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • What are theories for? What do they do?
  • What is a 'traditional' approach to foreign policy?
  • What are the central tenets of realism?
  • What are the central tenets of liberalism?
  • Are states functionally similar?
  • Are they ultimately rational actors?
  • What contribution do these theories make to our understanding of foreign policy?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 2: Traditional Theories of Foreign Policy Key Questions for seminar discussion: What are theories for? What do they do? What is a 'traditional' approach to foreign policy? What are the central tenets of realism? What are the central tenets of liberalism? Are states functionally similar? Are they ultimately rational actors? What contribution do these theories make to our understanding of foreign policy?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 4: Critical Theories of Foreign Policy

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • What does a 'critical' approach to foreign policy look like?
  • What are the central tenets of constructivism?
  • What are the central tenets of poststructuralism?
  • Are national interests socially constructed?
  • What work do representations of the world do?
  • Do these theories contribute to our understanding of foreign policy and diplomacy?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 4: Critical Theories of Foreign Policy Key Questions for seminar discussion: What does a 'critical' approach to foreign policy look like? What are the central tenets of constructivism? What are the central tenets of poststructuralism? Are national interests socially constructed? What work do representations of the world do? Do these theories contribute to our understanding of foreign policy and diplomacy?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 5: Making Foreign Policy

Key Questions for seminar discussion

  • Where does foreign policy come from?
  • What roles do different stakeholders- public, media, allies, international community- play in the development and practice of foreign policy?
  • Is foreign policy a site of negotiation and contestation?
  • How significant are these different actors in influencing foreign policy?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 5: Making Foreign Policy Key Questions for seminar discussion Where does foreign policy come from? What roles do different stakeholders- public, media, allies, international community- play in the development and practice of foreign policy? Is foreign policy a site of negotiation and contestation? How significant are these different actors in influencing foreign policy?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 6: Practices of Diplomacy

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • What are the key tools of diplomacy and statecraft?
  • Public Diplomacy Multilateralism, Bilateralism and Unilateralism
  • Coercive Diplomacy and the Threat of Force
  • In what contexts are these tools used? Are they effective?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 6: Practices of Diplomacy Key Questions for seminar discussion: What are the key tools of diplomacy and statecraft? Public Diplomacy Multilateralism, Bilateralism and Unilateralism Coercive Diplomacy and the Threat of Force In what contexts are these tools used? Are they effective?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 7: Case Study 1: China & the South China Sea, with Dr Jiye Kim

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • How does China approach the world?
  • What practices of diplomacy does China employ in the South China Sea?
  • How has this, and the process of foreign policy in China, changed over time on this issue?
  • What does China want to achieve in the region?
  • What options do other South China Sea claimants have in challenging Beijing?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK 7: Case Study 1: China & the South China Sea, with Dr Jiye Kim
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 7: Case Study 1: China & the South China Sea, with Dr Jiye Kim Key Questions for seminar discussion : How does China approach the world? What practices of diplomacy does China employ in the South China Sea? How has this, and the process of foreign policy in China, changed over time on this issue? What does China want to achieve in the region? What options do other South China Sea claimants have in challenging Beijing?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 8: Case Study 2: US & Cuban Missile Crisis

Key Questions for seminar discussion

  • The Cuban Missile Crisis: what happened?
  • What was the context?
  • What were the implications of the crisis?
  • What does this event tell us about US foreign policy? What lessons does this event have for Crisis Decision-Making?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK 8: Case Study 2: US & Cuban Missile Crisis
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 8: Case Study 2: US & Cuban Missile Crisis Key Questions for seminar discussion The Cuban Missile Crisis: what happened? What was the context? What were the implications of the crisis? What does this event tell us about US foreign policy? What lessons does this event have for Crisis Decision-Making?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 9: Case Study 3: Australia & Official Development Assistance

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • How does Australia view the world?
  • How has Australia viewed and approached Official Development Assistance?
  • What does Australia's ODA program tell us about Australia's approach to the Pacific region?
  • When and why do states recognise obligations beyond their borders?


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: WEEK 9: Case Study 3: Australia & Official Development Assistance
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 9:  Case Study 3: Australia & Official Development Assistance Key Questions for seminar discussion: How does Australia view the world? How has Australia viewed and approached Official Development Assistance? What does Australia's ODA program tell us about Australia's approach to the Pacific region? When and why do states recognise obligations beyond their borders?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 10: Case Study 4- Turkey & the West

Key Questions for seminar discussion:

  • What are the key drivers of Turkish foreign policy?
  • Is Turkey a 'torn' country?
  • Is Turkey a friend of the West or the West's 'other'?
  • What role does identity play in foreign policy?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 10: Case Study 4- Turkey & the West Key Questions for seminar discussion: What are the key drivers of Turkish foreign policy? Is Turkey a 'torn' country? Is Turkey a friend of the West or the West's 'other'? What role does identity play in foreign policy?

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 11: In Class Examination

A 90 minute in-person examination will be held in the normal seminar room from 8am on 14th October. See 'Assessment' for more details

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 12: Simulation Activity: Climate Change

This week will feature a simulation activity on the global politics of climate change, mapping on to diplomatic negotiations at the Conference of the Parties.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: WEEK 12: Simulation Activity: Climate Change This week will feature a simulation activity on the global politics of climate change, mapping on to diplomatic negotiations at the Conference of the Parties.

learning_activities.topic:
WEEK 13: Conclusion and Review

  • Reflecting on the origins of foreign policy: assumptions and global dynamics
  • Reflecting on the process of foreign policy formation and implementation
  • Key dynamics of diplomacy
  • The complexity of foreign policy Foreign policy in a changing world

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
